# Executive Technology Brief

**Date:** 2026-07-15

**Execution Time:** 03:00 AM (Local Company Time)

**Report ID:** ETB-2026-07-15-001

**Generated By:**
Technology Intelligence Routine

*Coverage note: this brief covers July 14–15, 2026. It resolves one open thread from the July 14 brief (Progress ShareFile's root cause is now confirmed as a path-traversal zero-day, patched in versions 5.12.5/6.0.2) and issues a correction to another (the "MCP vs. rival standard" framing from July 14 overstated the conflict — fuller reporting shows Google's A2A protocol and Anthropic's MCP are governed jointly under the Linux Foundation's new Agentic AI Foundation, with major vendors adopting both rather than choosing sides; see Section 7, Trend 6). It surfaces a genuinely heavy new-news day: the largest Patch Tuesday of the year with two zero-days already under active exploitation (SharePoint, Active Directory Federation Services); an active mass-exploitation campaign against Oracle PeopleSoft by ShinyHunters hitting 100+ organizations; a materially worse jscrambler npm supply-chain compromise than first reported; a confirmed Accenture source-code breach; OpenAI's GPT-5.6 reaching general availability with claimed coding-benchmark superiority over Claude; Apple's trade-secret lawsuit against OpenAI and its hardware chief; and a ~$1.7T AI/semiconductor stock selloff that puts this week's TSMC and ASML earnings in the spotlight as a referendum on the AI-capex and memory-supercycle theses this brief series has been tracking since June.*

---

# 1. Executive Snapshot

- **A RECORD-BREAKING PATCH TUESDAY SHIPPED TWO ZERO-DAYS ALREADY UNDER ACTIVE EXPLOITATION IN CORE IDENTITY AND COLLABORATION INFRASTRUCTURE — THIS IS TODAY'S SINGLE HIGHEST-URGENCY ITEM.** Microsoft's July cycle (570–622 CVEs depending on counting method, the largest of the year) permanently removed the Kerberos RC4 authentication rollback safety net as flagged in the July 14 brief, but also shipped fixes for CVE-2026-56164 (SharePoint, unauthenticated RCE) and CVE-2026-56155 (Active Directory Federation Services, elevation of privilege) — both already added to CISA's Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog with confirmed in-the-wild attacks. Separately, SAP's July Patch Day (previously pending) shipped a near-maximum-severity NetWeaver AS ABAP flaw (CVE-2026-44747, CVSS 9.9). Any organization running on-premises SharePoint, ADFS, or SAP NetWeaver should treat this as an active-incident-response priority today, not a routine patch cycle.

- **A CLUSTER OF ACTIVE, CONFIRMED-EXPLOITATION SUPPLY-CHAIN AND ENTERPRISE-SOFTWARE ATTACKS IS RUNNING RIGHT NOW, INDEPENDENT OF THE PATCH TUESDAY ITEMS.** ShinyHunters is actively chaining two Oracle PeopleSoft pre-auth RCEs (CVE-2026-35273, CVE-2026-35278) against 100+ organizations, including ~110 US education institutions, exfiltrating HR/payroll data. The compromised "jscrambler" npm package turns out to be materially worse than first reported: five malicious versions shipped over three hours, evading scanners after version 8.18.0, and specifically targeting AI coding-tool credentials (Claude Desktop, Cursor, Windsurf, Zed, VS Code) alongside cloud and crypto-wallet secrets — 1,479 downloads before full removal. Accenture has confirmed a breach after a threat actor claimed 35GB of source code, RSA/SSH keys, and Azure access tokens. Any org running PeopleSoft, any team that installed jscrambler 8.14.0–8.20.0 since July 11, and any Accenture client should treat exposure as live, not hypothetical.

- **PROGRESS SHAREFILE IS FULLY RESOLVED — PATCHED, ROOT CAUSE DISCLOSED — CLOSING THE THREAD FLAGGED IN THE LAST THREE BRIEFS.** The "credible external security threat" was a path-traversal zero-day in ShareFile Storage Zone Controller (all 5.x/6.x versions) allowing an authenticated admin-level attacker to read/write arbitrary files. Progress shipped patches (5.12.5, 6.0.2) and restored full service July 14. Any organization running on-prem ShareFile should patch immediately and can now stand down the emergency shutdown posture.

- **OPENAI'S GPT-5.6 REACHED GENERAL AVAILABILITY CLAIMING A CODING/AGENTIC BENCHMARK LEAD OVER CLAUDE, WHILE APPLE FILED A TRADE-SECRET LAWSUIT AGAINST OPENAI AND ITS HARDWARE CHIEF — THE FRONTIER AI COMPETITIVE AND LEGAL LANDSCAPE SHIFTED MEANINGFULLY THIS WEEK.** Third-party benchmarks put GPT-5.6 Sol ahead of Claude Fable 5 on the Artificial Analysis Coding Agent Index (80 vs. 77.2) using less than half the tokens/cost, and ahead on "Agents' Last Exam" by 13.1 points. Separately, Apple sued OpenAI and hardware chief Tang Tan (a 24-year Apple veteran) over an alleged two-year trade-secret theft scheme tied to OpenAI's in-development consumer hardware device. Any company benchmarking AI coding-assistant vendors should re-run cost/capability comparisons now that GPT-5.6 is generally available; any company relying on OpenAI's hardware roadmap should treat the lawsuit as a live continuity risk.

- **A ~$1.7T AI/SEMICONDUCTOR STOCK SELLOFF PUTS THIS WEEK'S TSMC AND ASML EARNINGS IN THE SPOTLIGHT AS A DIRECT TEST OF THE MEMORY-SUPERCYCLE AND AI-CAPEX THESES THIS BRIEF SERIES HAS TRACKED SINCE JUNE.** Micron and Intel both saw multi-day, multi-percent selloffs in July on renewed AI-bubble concerns (BofA's Bubble Risk Indicator hit 0.91, its highest since June 2000); Michael Burry disclosed a direct short on Micron. Complicating the memory-price bull case further, SK Hynix quietly postponed its HBM4 capacity ramp from Q2 to Q3 2026 to chase higher-margin conventional DRAM instead. TSMC reports July 16 and ASML reports July 15 — both are being explicitly framed by analysts as the market's referendum on whether AI infrastructure spending is sustainable. Any company modeling multi-year AI-infrastructure or cloud-compute budgets should treat this week's prints as a genuine inflection check, not routine earnings.

# 2. Technologies Worth Watching

---

### Technology 1: Record Patch Tuesday — Two Zero-Days Under Active Exploitation (SharePoint, ADFS) Added to CISA KEV

**Category:** Enterprise Solutions / Security / Cloud & Infrastructure

**What happened?**
Microsoft's July 14 Patch Tuesday shipped the largest CVE count of the year (reports range 570–622 depending on counting methodology, including 3 zero-days), on top of the previously-flagged permanent removal of the Kerberos RC4 authentication rollback registry key. Two of the zero-days are already confirmed under active exploitation and were added to CISA's Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog the same day: CVE-2026-56164 (SharePoint, missing-authentication RCE enabling IIS machine-key theft and malware deployment with no credentials required) and CVE-2026-56155 (Active Directory Federation Services, elevation of privilege). This is SharePoint's third KEV addition in 2026. Separately, a cluster of 21 filesystem-driver bugs (14 NTFS + 7 ReFS RCEs) suggests a shared underlying root cause.

**Why it matters?**
This converts last week's "audit before you patch" advisory into an active-exploitation emergency for two categories of infrastructure nearly every large enterprise runs: identity federation (ADFS) and document collaboration (SharePoint). CISA issued a same-day SharePoint hardening alert urging every server in a farm be patched (partial patching leaves exposure) plus a configuration-wizard re-run and IIS machine-key rotation post-compromise check.

**Business Value:** (1) Patch all on-premises SharePoint and ADFS servers immediately — treat as incident response, not scheduled maintenance, given confirmed in-the-wild exploitation. (2) Follow CISA's full hardening guidance for SharePoint farms, including machine-key rotation if compromise is suspected. (3) Complete the previously-flagged Kerberos RC4 dependency audit before applying the remainder of this cycle's updates to domain controllers.

**Company Relevance:** 70 / 100 (much higher for any organization running on-premises SharePoint or ADFS)

**Priority:** High

**Recommendation:** Adopt (immediate patch and hardening for SharePoint/ADFS; audit-then-patch for Kerberos RC4 as previously advised).

**Expected Impact:** Short-term (active exploitation risk this week); Long-term (reinforces that identity/collaboration infrastructure is a standing, recurring target class).

---

### Technology 2: SAP July Patch Day — Near-Maximum-Severity NetWeaver Flaw (CVSS 9.9)

**Category:** Enterprise Solutions / Security

**What happened?**
SAP's July Security Patch Day (previously pending at last brief's compilation time) published 16 new security notes plus updates, headlined by CVE-2026-44747 (NetWeaver AS ABAP memory corruption, CVSS 9.9, spanning multiple kernel release lines), CVE-2026-27690 (Approuter HTTP request smuggling, CVSS 9.1, unauthenticated), and CVE-2026-44761 (hardcoded sample credentials in Commerce Cloud, CVSS 9.1).

**Why it matters?**
NetWeaver is core ERP infrastructure for most large SAP deployments; a near-maximum-severity, unauthenticated-adjacent flaw in it is a crown-jewel risk, not a peripheral one. The hardcoded-credential issue in Commerce Cloud is a reminder that default/sample credentials remain a recurring, preventable class of enterprise-software risk.

**Business Value:** (1) Prioritize NetWeaver AS ABAP patching this week given the 9.9 severity score. (2) Audit Commerce Cloud deployments for the hardcoded sample credentials specifically — this is a configuration check as much as a patch. (3) Add SAP's second-Tuesday Patch Day to the same monthly review cadence as Microsoft's, since both landed on the same day this cycle.

**Company Relevance:** 40 / 100 (much higher — critical — for any organization running SAP NetWeaver or Commerce Cloud)

**Priority:** High (if SAP is in use)

**Recommendation:** Adopt immediately if applicable.

**Expected Impact:** Short-term (immediate patch priority); Long-term (reinforces ERP as a standing high-value target).

---

### Technology 3: Progress ShareFile — Resolved: Path-Traversal Zero-Day Confirmed and Patched

**Category:** Enterprise Solutions / Security

**What happened?**
The thread tracked across the last three briefs is now closed. Progress confirmed the underlying issue was a high-severity path-traversal zero-day in ShareFile Storage Zone Controller (all 5.x and 6.x versions), letting an authenticated admin-level attacker read/write arbitrary files and enumerate the server filesystem. Patches shipped as versions 5.12.5 and 6.0.2, and full cloud/on-prem service access was restored July 14. A CVE has been reserved but remains under embargo pending publication in roughly two weeks.

**Why it matters?**
This is a clean resolution: root cause disclosed, patch shipped, service restored. The one remaining caveat is that "no evidence of unauthorized access" is Progress's own claim, not yet independently verified by a third party — reasonable to trust but worth a light follow-up once the CVE publishes.

**Business Value:** (1) Patch any on-prem ShareFile Storage Zone Controller to 5.12.5/6.0.2 immediately — the emergency shutdown posture can now end, but only after patching, not before. (2) Confirm cloud ShareFile service is operating normally. (3) Revisit once the embargoed CVE publishes to confirm the severity score and whether any indicators of compromise were later found.

**Company Relevance:** 20 / 100 (much higher — critical — if ShareFile with on-prem components is in use)

**Priority:** Medium (down from High now that a patch exists and cloud/on-prem service is restored)

**Recommendation:** Adopt (patch immediately, then stand down elevated posture).

**Expected Impact:** Short-term (resolved once patched); closes a three-brief-long open thread.

---

### Technology 4: Active Mass-Exploitation of Oracle PeopleSoft by ShinyHunters (100+ Organizations)

**Category:** Enterprise Solutions / Security

**What happened?**
Oracle's July Critical Patch Update shipped a second PeopleSoft PeopleTools pre-authentication RCE (CVE-2026-35278, CVSS 9.8) alongside an earlier one (CVE-2026-35273). The ShinyHunters group (tracked as UNC6240) is actively chaining both against more than 100 organizations, including roughly 110 US education institutions, exfiltrating HR, payroll, and PII data from PeopleSoft HCM databases and using MeshCentral for command-and-control.

**Why it matters?**
This is a live, ongoing mass-exploitation campaign, not a disclosed-but-theoretical vulnerability — any internet-facing PeopleSoft/PeopleTools deployment is a real-time target today.

**Business Value:** (1) If PeopleSoft/PeopleTools is in use and internet-facing, patch to the July CPU immediately and treat as active-incident-response priority. (2) Audit for indicators of compromise regardless of patch status, given the scale of the ongoing campaign (100+ confirmed victims already). (3) Restrict PeopleSoft admin interfaces from public internet exposure as a standing hardening measure, independent of this specific CVE pair.

**Company Relevance:** 30 / 100 (critical if PeopleSoft is in use, especially in HR/education contexts)

**Priority:** High (if applicable)

**Recommendation:** Adopt immediately if PeopleSoft is in the environment.

**Expected Impact:** Short-term (active exploitation risk today).

---

### Technology 5: jscrambler npm Supply-Chain Attack — Broader Scope, Targeted AI Coding-Tool Credentials

**Category:** Developer Tools / Security

**What happened?**
The compromised "jscrambler" npm package, first reported as detected and pulled within six minutes, turns out to have had materially wider exposure. The attacker published five malicious versions (8.14.0 through 8.20.0) over roughly three hours, shifting technique after 8.18.0 from a preinstall hook (defeatable with `--ignore-scripts`) to a self-executing function embedded in the package's own code that fires on any import or CLI use — evading that mitigation. The malware specifically targeted cloud credentials (AWS/GCP/Azure), crypto wallets, and, notably, AI coding-assistant credentials for Claude Desktop, Cursor, Windsurf, Zed, and VS Code. Total exposure: 1,479 downloads of malicious versions before full removal; the clean version is 8.22.

**Why it matters?**
This is a concrete instance of a new, higher-value supply-chain attack target: credentials for AI coding assistants, which increasingly hold broad file-system and execution access. Organizations that treated "detected in 6 minutes" as low-risk should reassess given the actual three-hour, multi-version exposure window.

**Business Value:** (1) Confirm whether jscrambler versions 8.14.0–8.20.0 were installed anywhere in the environment since July 11; if so, rotate all cloud, CI, wallet, and AI coding-tool credentials in that environment immediately, not just npm-related ones. (2) Add "AI coding-assistant credentials" as an explicit category in supply-chain and credential-rotation runbooks going forward — this is the first concrete instance of that specific target class. (3) Reconfirm that `--ignore-scripts` alone is not a complete mitigation against this newer in-code-execution technique.

**Company Relevance:** 35 / 100 (higher for any team using jscrambler or with broad AI coding-tool adoption)

**Priority:** Medium-High

**Recommendation:** Adopt (verify exposure and rotate credentials immediately if any match found).

**Expected Impact:** Short-term (immediate credential-exposure risk if affected); Long-term (establishes AI coding-tool credentials as a named supply-chain target category).

---

### Technology 6: GPT-5.6 Reaches General Availability — OpenAI Claims Coding/Agentic Lead Over Claude

**Category:** Artificial Intelligence / Developer Tools

**What happened?**
OpenAI's GPT-5.6 family (Sol/Terra/Luna) went generally available July 9. Third-party benchmarks show Sol scoring 53.6 on "Agents' Last Exam" (13.1 points above Claude Fable 5), 80 on the Artificial Analysis Coding Agent Index (2.8 points above Fable 5, using less than half the tokens, time, and cost), 91.9% on Terminal-Bench 2.1, and 92.2% on BrowseComp.

**Why it matters?**
If these independently-reported benchmark numbers hold up under further scrutiny, OpenAI has retaken the coding/agentic lead from Anthropic on both raw capability and cost-efficiency — a direct, material input to any Claude-vs-GPT tooling or vendor decision, not incremental noise. This lands the same week Anthropic's Claude Fable 5 free-tier promo is set to expire (July 19) and metered pricing resumes.

**Business Value:** (1) Re-run any existing Claude-vs-GPT cost/capability benchmarking on representative internal coding or agentic tasks now that GPT-5.6 is GA, rather than relying on last quarter's comparison. (2) Factor the July 19 Fable 5 pricing reversion into near-term AI tooling cost projections regardless of which vendor is preferred. (3) Treat "Opus 5" rumors (a leaked "Honeycomb EAP" model briefly visible in Cursor's picker with a 1M-token context window) as unconfirmed — worth tracking, not a decision input yet.

**Company Relevance:** 55 / 100 (higher for any team with meaningful AI coding-assistant spend)

**Priority:** High

**Recommendation:** Pilot (re-benchmark before any new or renewed vendor commitment).

**Expected Impact:** Short-term (immediate re-evaluation opportunity); Medium-term (intensifies the OpenAI-Anthropic capability/price competition directly relevant to procurement timing).

---

### Technology 7: AI/Semiconductor Stock Rout Puts TSMC and ASML Earnings in the Spotlight

**Category:** Cloud & Infrastructure / Global Technology Companies

**What happened?**
A broad chip-stock selloff has erased roughly $1.7T in market capitalization, with Micron and Intel both posting multi-day, multi-percent declines in July on renewed AI-capex-bubble concerns; BofA's Bubble Risk Indicator hit 0.91 (highest since June 2000), and Michael Burry disclosed a direct short position on Micron. Complicating this brief series' prior memory-supercycle thesis further, SK Hynix quietly postponed its HBM4 capacity ramp from Q2 to Q3 2026, redirecting resources toward higher-margin conventional DRAM instead — and removed price caps from long-term supply contracts, diverging from Micron's approach. TSMC reports Q2 earnings July 16 (consensus ~$40B revenue, +36% YoY) and ASML reports July 15; both are being explicitly framed by analysts as the week's referendum on AI-infrastructure spending sustainability.

**Why it matters?**
This is a genuine complication, not a reversal, of the hardware cost-inflation signal flagged in the July 14 brief (Micron's then-record earnings). The signal is now mixed: earnings still show record AI-driven revenue, but equity markets are pricing meaningfully higher risk into the same names, and the largest HBM supplier is deliberately slowing its AI-specific capacity ramp in favor of near-term margin. Any budget model built purely on the "unlimited AI-infrastructure demand, guaranteed multi-year price inflation" thesis should be stress-tested against this week's actual TSMC/ASML results.

**Business Value:** (1) Treat this week's TSMC (July 16) and ASML (July 15) earnings as a genuine checkpoint for any multi-year AI-infrastructure or cloud-compute budget assumption, not routine reporting to skim. (2) Do not assume SK Hynix's HBM4 slowdown eases memory pricing broadly — it reflects a margin-optimization choice, not reduced underlying demand, and conventional DRAM pricing pressure continues. (3) Revisit any GPU-procurement or cloud-capacity negotiation timing in light of both the compute-scarcity signals (Google rationing Gemini compute to Meta, Google paying SpaceX $920M/month for bridge GPU capacity) and this equity-market volatility — the two are not contradictory, but together suggest genuine uncertainty about near-term AI-infrastructure economics.

**Company Relevance:** 35 / 100 (higher for any company with significant cloud AI-compute spend or planned on-premises AI hardware investment)

**Priority:** Medium

**Recommendation:** Observe (stress-test budget models against this week's earnings before finalizing any multi-year commitment).

**Expected Impact:** Short-term (this week's earnings are a direct signal); Medium-term (determines whether the memory-supercycle thesis holds through Q4 2026 as previously modeled).

---

### Technology 8: Correction — MCP and Google's A2A Are Complementary, Governed Jointly, Not Rival Standards

**Category:** Developer Ecosystem / Enterprise Solutions / Artificial Intelligence

**What happened?**
The July 14 brief reported Google, Microsoft, Salesforce, Snowflake, and ServiceNow as backing a "rival standard" to Anthropic's MCP. Fuller reporting available now clarifies this framing was overstated. The "rival" is specifically Google's A2A (Agent2Agent) protocol, donated to the Linux Foundation and now at v1.2 with roughly 150 organizations in production. Critically, A2A and MCP now sit under the same governance umbrella — the newly formed Agentic AI Foundation (AAIF) — as separate but complementary projects, with the coalition backing it expanded beyond the five companies named July 14 to also include Cisco, Databricks, GitHub, GoDaddy, Hugging Face, and NVIDIA. Multiple outlets now frame the relationship as "MCP for sharing context, A2A for dynamic interaction among agents" — most major platforms (Salesforce, SAP, IBM, Microsoft, AWS) are committing to both rather than picking sides. Separately, MCP's July 28 spec revision now has concrete detail: the stateless-core change is formalized as SEP-2567, and the Enterprise-Managed Authorization (EMA) extension for zero-touch SSO is now stable, adopted by Anthropic, Microsoft, and Okta, with server-side support from Asana, Atlassian, Canva, Figma, Granola, Linear, and Supabase.

**Why it matters?**
This meaningfully lowers the protocol-fragmentation risk flagged in the July 14 brief. Rather than a binary choice between competing standards, the practical reality is that MCP (context/tool access) and A2A (agent-to-agent orchestration) solve different problems and are being adopted together by the same major vendors. Companies that paused MCP-exclusive investment pending clarity on this "standards war" can resume with more confidence, though the two-protocol reality still means both should be accounted for in any agent-integration architecture going forward.

**Business Value:** (1) Resume or continue MCP-based integration work with reduced fragmentation risk — this is not a "pick a side" decision. (2) For any team building multi-agent orchestration (agents calling other agents, not just agents calling tools), evaluate A2A as a complementary layer rather than a competing one. (3) Diff any live MCP integration against the SEP-2567 stateless-core change and the new EMA authorization extension ahead of the July 28 finalization — both are concrete, dated changes worth reviewing now.

**Company Relevance:** 30 / 100 (higher for any team building AI-agent-to-enterprise-software integrations)

**Priority:** Medium

**Recommendation:** Pilot (resume MCP investment; evaluate A2A for multi-agent orchestration use cases specifically).

**Expected Impact:** Medium-term (clarifies integration-architecture decisions); corrects and lowers the vendor-strategy risk flagged in the prior brief.

# 3. New Tools & Platforms

---

### Cursor 3.11 — Side Chats, Grok 4.5 as Default Model Across All Plans

**Category:** Engineering

**What does it do?** Adds Side Chats (durable secondary agent conversations via `/side` or `/btw` that run without interrupting the main session), local agent-transcript search, redesigned project/repo pickers, five new cloud-agent hook events, and Team MCP distribution. Grok 4.5 — xAI's coding-tuned model, co-developed with Cursor — is now the default model across all Cursor plans (desktop, web, iOS, CLI, SDK), not an optional add-on.

**Who should use it?** Engineering teams already on Cursor, and any team benchmarking coding-assistant cost/capability now that Grok 4.5 is the out-of-the-box default rather than an opt-in choice.

**Business Use Case:** Making Grok 4.5 the default (at $2/$6 per million input/output tokens, cited as materially cheaper than Opus 4.8) changes the baseline cost profile for any team on Cursor without requiring explicit configuration — worth confirming this doesn't silently shift output quality or behavior on existing workflows.

**Estimated ROI:** Moderate — mainly a cost-profile shift for existing users; confirm quality parity before treating it as a straightforward win.

**Recommended Experiment:** Compare output quality on a representative coding task between the new Grok 4.5 default and whatever model was previously pinned, given the default changed without an explicit opt-in.

**Estimated Evaluation Time:** 3–5 days.

---

### Salesforce Agentforce Pay-Per-Resolution — Now Generally Available

**Category:** Automation / AI

**What does it do?** Salesforce's outcome-based Agentforce Help Agent pricing (pay only when an AI agent fully resolves a case; $0 if escalated to a human or rated negatively) reached general availability. Salesforce claims Help Agent autonomously resolved 70% of 4.3 million inquiries on its own help portal.

**Who should use it?** Any customer-support or service organization currently paying per-seat or per-consumption for AI agent tooling, especially where resolution rates are high enough to make outcome-based pricing favorable.

**Business Use Case:** True outcome-based pricing removes the risk of paying for AI agent capacity that doesn't actually resolve anything — directly relevant to any team evaluating Agentforce against Microsoft Copilot, ServiceNow (which moved to token-bundle tiers, not true outcome-based pricing), or Zendesk.

**Estimated ROI:** Potentially high for high-volume, well-scoped support workloads; dependent on actual resolution rate matching Salesforce's cited 70% figure in your own environment, not assumed.

**Recommended Experiment:** Pilot Agentforce Help Agent against one well-defined support queue for 30 days and compare cost-per-resolution against current tooling.

**Estimated Evaluation Time:** 30 days.

---

### Kubernetes 1.35 — Dynamic Resource Allocation Reaches GA

**Category:** Cloud / Engineering

**What does it do?** Dynamic Resource Allocation (DRA), the mechanism for fine-grained scheduling of GPUs and other accelerators across a Kubernetes cluster, graduated to general availability. NVIDIA has moved its GPU driver for DRA into the Kubernetes SIG ecosystem directly.

**Who should use it?** Any team running GPU-heavy AI/ML workloads on Kubernetes, particularly where GPU utilization efficiency matters given the current compute-scarcity and cost-inflation environment flagged elsewhere in this brief.

**Business Use Case:** Better GPU scheduling efficiency directly offsets rising per-GPU costs — relevant to any on-prem or hybrid AI-infrastructure investment being planned this quarter.

**Estimated ROI:** Meaningful for GPU-constrained environments; low priority if not running GPU workloads on Kubernetes.

**Recommended Experiment:** If already on Kubernetes for AI/ML workloads, evaluate DRA against current GPU-scheduling approach for a measurable utilization-rate improvement.

**Estimated Evaluation Time:** 1–2 weeks.

---

### Docker Hardened Images — Over 1,000 Now Free

**Category:** Engineering / Security

**What does it do?** Docker made its previously paid-tier catalog of hardened, minimal-CVE container base images (1,000+) free and open to all users, alongside Docker Compose improvements and two CVE fixes.

**Who should use it?** Any engineering team currently building on unhardened or self-maintained base images, or paying for a hardened-image subscription elsewhere.

**Business Use Case:** Reduces both cost and baseline CVE exposure in container images with effectively no migration cost — a low-effort, immediate hardening win.

**Estimated ROI:** High relative to cost — free, drop-in reduction in base-image attack surface.

**Recommended Experiment:** Swap base images for 2–3 actively maintained services to Docker's hardened equivalents and confirm no compatibility issues.

**Estimated Evaluation Time:** 1–2 days.

---

### MCP 2026-07-28 Release Candidate — SEP-2567 Stateless Core, EMA Authorization Now Stable

**Category:** Engineering / AI

**What does it do?** MCP's largest-ever spec revision remains on track for July 28. The stateless-core change is now formalized as SEP-2567 (removes the `Mcp-Session-Id` header and `initialize` handshake in favor of per-request metadata). Six additional SEPs harden OAuth/OIDC. The Enterprise-Managed Authorization (EMA) extension for zero-touch SSO is now stable, with server-side support from Asana, Atlassian, Canva, Figma, Granola, Linear, and Supabase. Roots, Sampling, and Logging capabilities are being deprecated on a 12-month minimum removal window — a new detail not previously flagged.

**Who should use it?** Any team with a live MCP-based agent integration, now with reduced standards-fragmentation risk per the correction in Section 2.

**Business Use Case:** Avoids a breaking-change surprise on July 28 while unlocking zero-touch enterprise SSO for MCP-based tools via EMA.

**Estimated ROI:** High relative to cost — reviewing the spec diff now is far cheaper than debugging a broken integration on release day.

**Recommended Experiment:** Diff any existing MCP integration against the SEP-2567 changes and the EMA authorization extension before July 28; flag any usage of Roots, Sampling, or Logging capabilities given the new deprecation timeline.

**Estimated Evaluation Time:** 2–4 hours per integration.

---

### Ollama — Continued Growth, Interactive Agent Mode Added

**Category:** AI / Engineering

**What does it do?** No material change to the funding/adoption picture since the July 9 $65M Series B (8.9M monthly developers, 85% Fortune 500 penetration, $88M total raised). Product update: v0.32.0 added an interactive agent mode (chat/code/web-search/delegation) directly at the CLI entry point, and roughly 90% faster Gemma 4 inference on Apple Silicon via multi-token prediction.

**Who should use it?** Teams evaluating a hedge against per-token API costs or closed-model vendor lock-in, now with a more capable local agent-mode option.

**Business Use Case:** The interactive agent mode narrows the capability gap between local open-weight models and hosted agent tooling for well-defined tasks — increasingly relevant given this week's confirmed hardware cost volatility.

**Estimated ROI:** Workload-dependent; meaningful for high-volume, low-complexity tasks currently paying per-token API rates.

**Recommended Experiment:** Migrate one internal, non-customer-facing AI workload to Ollama's new agent mode and compare cost and quality over two weeks. (Carried over unaddressed from three prior briefs — recommend actually starting this pilot.)

**Estimated Evaluation Time:** 2 weeks.

# 4. Global Technology Direction

### Where is the market moving?

**July 15, 2026: Two narratives are colliding this week — confirmed, active exploitation across enterprise infrastructure on one side, and a genuine market re-pricing of AI-infrastructure economics on the other — while the AI capability race reshuffled and a standards conflict flagged yesterday turned out to be overstated.** On the security side, this is one of the heaviest weeks in this brief series: a record Patch Tuesday with two zero-days already under active exploitation (SharePoint, ADFS), a near-maximum-severity SAP NetWeaver flaw, an active mass-exploitation campaign against Oracle PeopleSoft (100+ victims), a materially worse npm supply-chain compromise than first reported, and a confirmed Accenture breach — all landing in the same 48-hour window, even as the ShareFile thread finally closed cleanly. On the economics side, a ~$1.7T chip-stock selloff and SK Hynix's HBM4 ramp slowdown complicate — without fully reversing — the hardware cost-inflation thesis Micron's earnings confirmed just one day earlier, with TSMC and ASML earnings this week serving as the next real data point. On the capability side, GPT-5.6's GA launch with claimed benchmark superiority over Claude, alongside Apple's trade-secret lawsuit against OpenAI, add real competitive and legal uncertainty to the frontier-lab landscape. And on standards, yesterday's "MCP vs. rival standard" framing turned out to be substantially overstated — MCP and Google's A2A are complementary protocols under joint governance, not competing bets.

**Major company movements:**

- **Microsoft:** Shipped the year's largest Patch Tuesday (570–622 CVEs, 3 zero-days, 2 under active exploitation and added to CISA KEV) alongside the previously-flagged permanent Kerberos RC4 rollback removal; continues substituting in-house MAI models for OpenAI/Anthropic in Copilot to cut costs (Mustafa Suleyman: "our goal is to reduce and ultimately eliminate" third-party model spend); is a founding backer of the Agentic AI Foundation alongside Google, now clarified as complementary to, not opposed to, Anthropic's MCP.

- **OpenAI:** GPT-5.6 (Sol/Terra/Luna) reached general availability July 9 with claimed coding/agentic benchmark superiority over Claude; Apple filed a trade-secret lawsuit July 10 against OpenAI and hardware chief Tang Tan over an alleged two-year scheme to steal confidential Apple hardware/manufacturing designs for OpenAI's in-development consumer device; $1T IPO valuation ambition remains in tension with a 2027-not-2026 listing timeline and a $40B SoftBank bridge loan due March 2027; a proposed 5% US government equity stake (~$42.6B) remains a live, unresolved policy question.

- **Anthropic:** Claude Fable 5's free-tier access is confirmed reverting to metered billing July 19 after a third extension, reinforcing the compute-capacity constraint signal; reportedly in early-stage talks with Samsung for a custom 2nm inference chip; annualized revenue run-rate reportedly reached ~$69B by mid-July per third-party estimates (up sharply from $47B at its Series H weeks earlier, though not yet company-confirmed); public S-1 filing and Nasdaq listing remain on track for roughly September/October 2026 at a valuation approaching $1T; the Claude Code/China MIIT "backdoor" dispute remains unresolved, with Alibaba's company-wide ban now confirmed in effect since July 10.

- **Google/DeepMind:** Gemini 3.5 Pro's ~July 17 target remains unconfirmed by Google, with reporting suggesting a full base-model rebuild after "structural failures" were found in recursive tool-calling; Google is rationing Gemini compute allocated to Meta amid industry-wide GPU scarcity, while simultaneously paying SpaceX $920M/month for 110,000 Nvidia GPUs as bridge capacity; A2A protocol (donated to the Linux Foundation, now v1.2, ~150 organizations in production) is clarified as complementary to MCP, not a rival, under the new Agentic AI Foundation.

- **Meta:** Custom "Iris" AI chip (with Broadcom, built by TSMC) confirmed on track for September 2026 production after six weeks of clean internal testing; separately preparing "Meta Compute," a unit to resell excess AI training/inference capacity to enterprises, echoing SpaceX's compute-monetization approach; acquired robotics/embodied-AI startup Assured Robot Intelligence.

- **xAI/SpaceXAI/Cursor:** The reported $60B all-stock acquisition of Cursor/Anysphere remains on track for a Q3 2026 close, with a joint SpaceXAI-Cursor coding model already live ahead of formal deal completion; Grok 4.5 remains blocked in the EU pending AI Act systemic-risk compliance, with only an unconfirmed "mid-July" rollout target.

- **Nvidia:** Disputed a SemiAnalysis report claiming Rubin-platform delays; all six Rubin chips reportedly passed initial fab testing and the platform is now tracking roughly two quarters ahead of its original H2 FY2027 schedule, a bullish signal partially offsetting this week's broader chip-sector equity weakness.

- **Enterprise software:** Salesforce's Agentforce pay-per-resolution pricing reached general availability (70% autonomous resolution claimed on its own help portal); ServiceNow responded with consumption-based (not true outcome-based) tiered pricing and raised its Now Assist ARR target from $1B to $1.5B mid-quarter; Google Cloud's Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform launched as a direct competitor with a $750M partner innovation fund; SAP completed its Dremio acquisition while simultaneously restricting hiring and travel spend to fund its AI push; a California federal court allowed a bias lawsuit against Workday's AI hiring-screening tools to proceed past dismissal — a first substantive test of HR-tech vendor liability for AI screening outcomes.

- **Security landscape:** Two Patch Tuesday zero-days added to CISA KEV (SharePoint, ADFS); SAP's critical NetWeaver flaw (CVSS 9.9); Oracle PeopleSoft under active mass exploitation by ShinyHunters (100+ organizations); jscrambler npm compromise confirmed materially worse than first reported, specifically targeting AI coding-tool credentials; Accenture confirmed a breach after a 35GB source-code theft claim; ShareFile's thread fully resolved (patched, root cause disclosed); GhostApproval (Windsurf, Augment) and GitLost (GitHub Agentic Workflows) both remain unresolved with no vendor movement this window.

- **Regulatory:** China's "Interim Measures for the Administration of AI Anthropomorphic Interactive Services" took full effect July 15, with ByteDance's Doubao (data-export deadline October 15) and Alibaba's Qwen (no migration path, immediate deletion) both preemptively compliant; a separate EU Cloud and AI Development Act published in the Official Journal the same day, establishing a tiered EU cloud-sovereignty framework phasing in from February 2028; New York became the first US state to impose a moratorium on new hyperscale data centers over 50MW; the FTC's Section 5 "AI accuracy" comment period remains open through July 31, with new detail that its proposed policy statement explicitly argues state AI laws (naming Colorado's) may be federally preempted.

**Industry shifts:**

1. **Enterprise infrastructure is under simultaneous, active attack across multiple unrelated vectors this week.** Two Patch Tuesday zero-days under active exploitation, a critical SAP flaw, an active PeopleSoft mass-exploitation campaign, a worse-than-reported npm supply-chain compromise, and a confirmed Accenture breach landed within roughly 48 hours — this is a genuinely elevated-severity week, not five unrelated minor stories.

2. **The AI-infrastructure economics story is genuinely mixed, not one-directional, for the first time in this brief series.** Micron's record earnings (July 14) confirmed hardware cost inflation as hard fact; one day later, a ~$1.7T equity selloff and SK Hynix's deliberate HBM4 ramp slowdown complicate the "unlimited demand, guaranteed price inflation" thesis. Both can be true simultaneously — treat this week's TSMC/ASML earnings as the next real data point, not a settled question either way.

3. **A standards conflict flagged as a risk yesterday turned out to be substantially overstated on further reporting.** MCP and Google's A2A are governed jointly under a new industry-wide foundation and solve complementary problems, not competing ones — a reminder that first-wave reporting on multi-vendor coalition announcements can overstate conflict before governance details clarify.

4. **AI coding-tool credentials are now a confirmed, named target for supply-chain attacks.** The jscrambler compromise specifically targeted Claude Desktop, Cursor, Windsurf, Zed, and VS Code credentials — the first concrete instance of this attack pattern, following the GhostApproval and GitLost disclosures earlier this month that established AI-agent trust boundaries as an exploitable surface generally.

5. **Enterprise AI governance is not keeping pace with enterprise AI deployment.** New survey data shows 97% of executives report deploying AI agents in the past year, but only roughly one in five companies has a mature governance model for them, and 79% report adoption challenges — a widening execution/governance gap independent of any single vendor or incident.

### What This Means For Our Company

1. **Patch SharePoint, ADFS, and (if applicable) SAP NetWeaver and Oracle PeopleSoft immediately** — all four carry confirmed or near-maximum-severity active-exploitation risk this week, ahead of the routine Kerberos RC4 audit already in progress from the July 14 brief.

2. **Confirm whether jscrambler 8.14.0–8.20.0 was installed anywhere since July 11, and rotate cloud, CI, wallet, and AI coding-tool credentials if so** — this is a materially larger exposure window than first reported, and specifically targets AI coding-assistant credentials.

3. **Patch any on-prem ShareFile Storage Zone Controller to 5.12.5/6.0.2 and stand down the emergency shutdown posture** — this thread is now cleanly resolved.

4. **Re-benchmark AI coding-assistant vendors given GPT-5.6's GA launch and claimed benchmark lead over Claude**, and factor the July 19 Fable 5 pricing reversion into near-term cost projections regardless of vendor choice.

5. **Resume or continue MCP-based integration investment with reduced fragmentation concern**, while evaluating Google's A2A as a complementary layer for multi-agent orchestration use cases specifically — this is not a "pick a side" decision as the July 14 brief's framing implied.

---

# 6. CTO Decision Board

### START THIS WEEK

1. **Patch SharePoint (CVE-2026-56164) and ADFS (CVE-2026-56155) immediately** — both are under confirmed active exploitation and on CISA's Known Exploited Vulnerabilities list; treat as incident response, not scheduled maintenance.

2. **Patch SAP NetWeaver and Oracle PeopleSoft if either is in the environment**, and audit for jscrambler 8.14.0–8.20.0 exposure with credential rotation if found — all three carry active or near-maximum-severity risk this week.

3. **Patch on-prem ShareFile Storage Zone Controllers to 5.12.5/6.0.2** and complete the Kerberos RC4 dependency audit already in progress from the July 14 brief.

### EVALUATE THIS MONTH

1. **Re-benchmark AI coding-assistant vendors now that GPT-5.6 is GA and Claude Fable 5's free-tier pricing reverts July 19** — treat current vendor assumptions as stale.

2. **Stress-test AI-infrastructure budget models against this week's TSMC/ASML earnings and the SK Hynix HBM4 ramp slowdown**, rather than relying solely on last week's Micron-earnings-driven cost-inflation assumption.

3. **Actually start the Ollama pilot** for one internal workload — this has now carried over unaddressed across four consecutive briefs.

### IGNORE FOR NOW

1. **Gemini 3.5 Pro's rumored ~July 17 launch date** — still unconfirmed by Google, reportedly delayed by a base-model rebuild; do not plan around it.

2. **The specific "MCP vs. rival standard" framing from the July 14 brief** — now corrected; do not restructure integration architecture around a standards conflict that fuller reporting shows is largely overstated.

3. **The Apple v. OpenAI lawsuit and OpenAI's proposed government equity stake as immediate operational concerns** — both are real developments worth tracking as vendor-durability signals, but neither yet warrants a change to any current OpenAI tooling relationship.

# 7. Trend Detection

*Compared against ETB-2026-06-16 through ETB-2026-07-14*

---

**Trend 1: "AI Agent With Elevated Access" Attack Surface Widens to Include Supply-Chain Credential Theft (Escalation of Trend 1, ETB-2026-07-14)**

The July 14 brief established GhostApproval and GitLost as two mechanically distinct instances of AI agents with elevated file/repository access acting on attacker-controlled input. This window adds a third, distinct mechanism: the jscrambler npm compromise specifically targeted AI coding-assistant credentials (Claude Desktop, Cursor, Windsurf, Zed, VS Code) as a named theft objective — not an agent being tricked into misbehaving, but a direct attack on the credentials that grant agents their elevated access in the first place. Three unrelated mechanisms (symlink confusion, prompt injection, credential-targeting malware) now converge on the same underlying pattern: AI coding tools with broad system access are a confirmed, valuable, and actively-targeted attack surface.

**Confidence:** 90% (up from 88% — a third, mechanically distinct instance within a week is strong evidence this is now a standing attacker priority, not a coincidence)

**Suggested Action:** Extend the standing security-review category from "AI agent with elevated access acting on untrusted input" to explicitly include "AI coding-tool credentials as a supply-chain theft target" — this requires credential-rotation runbooks to treat AI tool credentials with the same priority as cloud and CI credentials, which most do not yet do.

---

**Trend 2: Enterprise Infrastructure Faces Simultaneous, Unrelated Active-Exploitation Campaigns (New Trend)**

This window surfaced four independent, confirmed active-exploitation situations landing within roughly 48 hours: two Patch Tuesday zero-days (SharePoint, ADFS) added to CISA KEV, an active Oracle PeopleSoft mass-exploitation campaign (100+ victims), a confirmed Accenture breach, and the wider-than-reported jscrambler compromise. These are mechanically and organizationally unrelated — different vendors, different attack classes, different threat actors — but their simultaneity is itself a data point about the current threat environment's intensity.

**Confidence:** 65% (new trend, single-window observation with four independent confirming data points — needs at least one more week of comparably-elevated activity before treating "simultaneous multi-vector active exploitation" as a recurring pattern rather than one unusually heavy week)

**Suggested Action:** Track whether the coming week shows a similarly high concentration of confirmed active-exploitation incidents (versus disclosed-but-unexploited vulnerabilities); if so, consider this a standing elevated-baseline threat environment rather than an anomalous week and adjust patch-cycle SLAs accordingly.

---

**Trend 3: The Three-Way Capability/Economics/Trust-Stability Framework Gains a Genuine Economics Complication (Evolution of Trend 4, ETB-2026-07-14)**

The July 14 brief reinforced all three dimensions of its capability/economics/trust-stability framework using Micron's then-record earnings as unambiguous confirmation of hardware cost inflation. This window complicates — without reversing — the economics dimension specifically: a ~$1.7T chip-stock selloff and SK Hynix's deliberate HBM4 ramp slowdown introduce genuine two-sided uncertainty into what had been a one-directional "AI infrastructure costs will keep rising" narrative. Capability shifted meaningfully too (GPT-5.6's GA launch and claimed lead over Claude), and trust/stability saw both a resolution (ShareFile) and new complications (jscrambler, Accenture, Apple v. OpenAI).

**Confidence:** 78% (up from 74% — all three dimensions remain simultaneously active, but the economics dimension is now genuinely two-sided rather than one-directional, which is itself informative)

**Suggested Action:** When modeling AI-infrastructure budgets, explicitly carry a range (not a point estimate) for hardware/compute cost trajectories through Q4 2026, given this week's genuine market disagreement between hard earnings data and equity pricing.

---

**Trend 4: Standards-Fragmentation Fears Are Resolving Toward Coexistence, Not Conflict (Correction of Trend 6, ETB-2026-07-14)**

The July 14 brief flagged a new trend at 55% confidence: simultaneous consolidation (SpaceXAI/Cursor) and fragmentation (MCP vs. a rival standards bloc) in the AI-coding-tool ecosystem. This window's fuller reporting resolves the fragmentation half of that trend in the opposite direction from what was flagged — MCP and Google's A2A are governed jointly under the new Agentic AI Foundation and adopted together, not competitively, by the same major vendors. The consolidation half (SpaceXAI/Cursor) remains on track and unresolved as a separate, still-live thread.

**Confidence:** 70% (a genuine correction, not merely an update — the underlying coalition and governance structure is now documented across multiple independent sources, meaningfully higher confidence than the single-window 55% flagged initially)

**Suggested Action:** Retire "standards fragmentation" as an active vendor-risk concern for MCP-based work specifically; continue tracking the SpaceXAI/Cursor acquisition close (expected Q3 2026) as the separate, still-open consolidation-risk thread.

---

**Trend 5: US–China AI Tooling Decoupling Reaches Its Scheduled Regulatory Milestone, No New Escalation (Continuation of Trend 5, ETB-2026-07-14)**

China's "Interim Measures for the Administration of AI Anthropomorphic Interactive Services" took full effect July 15 as scheduled, with ByteDance's Doubao and Alibaba's Qwen both already preemptively compliant. The Claude Code/MIIT dispute and Alibaba's employee ban remain in the same unresolved standoff described in the July 14 brief, with no new developments this window.

**Confidence:** 82% (up slightly from 80% — the scheduled regulatory milestone landing exactly on time, with both major Chinese platforms already compliant, reinforces the pattern of China-side AI governance proceeding on a predictable schedule)

**Suggested Action:** Continue monitoring for resolution of the Claude Code/MIIT standoff specifically; no new action required this week beyond continued tracking.

---

**Trend 6 (New): Enterprise AI Governance Is Not Keeping Pace With Enterprise AI Deployment**

New survey data (Writer, industry analyst commentary) shows 97% of executives report their organization deployed AI agents in the past year, but only roughly one in five companies has a mature governance model for those agents, and 79% report AI-adoption challenges — a widening gap between deployment speed and governance maturity that is distinct from, but related to, the security-focused "AI agent with elevated access" trend tracked above.

**Confidence:** 60% (new trend, based on survey data rather than a specific incident — worth tracking for corroboration via additional survey sources or concrete governance-failure incidents before treating as fully established)

**Suggested Action:** Use this as an internal prompt to assess whether current AI-agent deployments have documented approval scope, data-access boundaries, and audit trails — independent of any specific vendor risk, this is a standing organizational-maturity question worth a periodic check.

---

# 8. Strategic Roadmap

### THIS MONTH (July 15 – July 31, 2026)

- **Today (July 15):** ASML reports Q2 earnings; patch SharePoint (CVE-2026-56164) and ADFS (CVE-2026-56155) immediately given confirmed active exploitation; China's Anthropomorphic AI Interactive Services rules take full effect; EU Cloud and AI Development Act publishes in the Official Journal (formal effect August 4, phased obligations from February 2028).
- **July 16:** TSMC reports Q2 earnings — the week's key AI-capex/memory-supercycle checkpoint; GitHub Models runs a planned brownout ahead of its July 30 full retirement.
- **~July 17 (unconfirmed):** Gemini 3.5 Pro's rumored launch window — not Google-confirmed; do not plan around this date.
- **July 19:** Claude Fable 5's free-tier access under paid plans reverts to standard metered billing.
- **This week:** Confirm jscrambler 8.14.0–8.20.0 exposure and rotate credentials if found; patch SAP NetWeaver and Oracle PeopleSoft if applicable; patch ShareFile Storage Zone Controllers to 5.12.5/6.0.2; complete the Kerberos RC4 dependency audit.
- **July 23:** GitHub Models runs a second planned brownout ahead of retirement.
- **July 28:** MCP 2026-07-28 spec finalizes (SEP-2567 stateless core, EMA authorization extension, tightened OAuth/OIDC) — review any live MCP integration against the release candidate beforehand.
- **July 29–30:** KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Japan (Yokohama) — relevant for any team tracking Kubernetes 1.35 DRA adoption.
- **July 30:** GitHub Models fully retires.
- **July 31:** FTC's public comment period closes on its proposed Section 5 "AI accuracy" policy statement, including its preemption argument against state AI laws.
- **This month:** Re-benchmark AI coding-assistant vendors given GPT-5.6's GA launch; stress-test AI-infrastructure budgets against this week's TSMC/ASML earnings; start (not merely plan) the Ollama pilot; assess AI-agent governance maturity internally.

### NEXT 90 DAYS (July–September 2026)

- **August 1:** Formal deadline for the White House's voluntary frontier-model framework design.
- **August 4:** EU Cloud and AI Development Act formally takes effect (substantive tiered obligations phase in starting February 2028).
- **August 9:** OpenAI's standalone Atlas browser is fully discontinued, folded into the ChatGPT desktop app and a Chrome extension.
- **August 31:** Claude Sonnet 5 introductory pricing expires; GitHub Copilot's temporary AI-credit billing cushion expires; EU AI Act transparency obligations take effect for in-scope systems.
- **September 2026:** Meta's custom "Iris" AI chip enters production (Broadcom/TSMC-built, part of a 14GW compute buildout).
- **September 15:** Cloudflare's AI-crawler blocking regime shifts to new defaults (Agent and Training categories blocked by default on ad-monetized pages; Search remains allowed by default).
- **~September (est.):** Anthropic's public S-1 release reportedly targeted, ahead of an ~October 2026 IPO at a valuation approaching $1T.
- **Q3 2026:** Watch for the reported SpaceXAI/Cursor acquisition to close; watch for the embargoed ShareFile CVE to publish with full severity detail; watch for TSMC/ASML earnings follow-through on the AI-capex sustainability question raised this week.

### NEXT 12 MONTHS (H2 2026 – H1 2027)

- **2027 (directional):** OpenAI's IPO timeline reportedly slipping to 2027, in tension with SoftBank's March 25, 2027 loan-repayment deadline; Databricks targeting a 2027 IPO (reportedly in talks for a new private round at $165–175B valuation, potentially closing as soon as July 2026).
- **Ongoing:** Track whether "AI agent/coding-tool credential theft" incidents continue recurring across additional vendors and supply-chain vectors; whether the enterprise AI governance gap (only ~1 in 5 companies mature) narrows or widens; whether GhostApproval's unpatched vendors (Windsurf, Augment) or GitLost (GitHub Agentic Workflows) see any vendor movement.
- **December 2026:** EU AI Act Omnibus adds a new prohibition on AI-generated non-consensual sexual/intimate imagery and CSAM.
- **December 2, 2027:** EU AI Act high-risk (Annex III) obligations take effect (deferred from August 2026 per the May 2026 Omnibus agreement).

OpenAI lawsuit's specific factual allegations (based on Apple's complaint as reported by press, not yet tested in court). Lower confidence, explicitly flagged: Gemini 3.5 Pro's ~July 17 date (still unconfirmed by Google); claims of an xAI "Grok Build" launch and Zhipu GLM 5.2 coding-model claims circulating in aggregator summaries (could not independently verify with a primary source this window — excluded from the body of this brief pending confirmation); a widely-repeated claim of a July 14 Google Cloud "Gemini Enterprise" announcement appears to conflate it with an earlier April 2026 event and was excluded accordingly.)*

---

*If we only execute one thing today, it should be: **Patch SharePoint (CVE-2026-56164) and Active Directory Federation Services (CVE-2026-56155) immediately.** Both are already on CISA's Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog with confirmed in-the-wild attacks against core identity and collaboration infrastructure that nearly every large organization runs — this is not a scheduled-maintenance item, it is an active-incident-response priority that should take precedence over every other item in this brief, including the Kerberos RC4 audit already in progress.*
